bibliographic.citation: James Orchard Halliwell, ed., The Thornton Romances: The Early English Metrical Romances of Perceval, Isumbras, Eglamour, and Degrevant, Selected from Manuscripts at Lincoln and Cambridge (London: printed for the Camden Society by John Bowyer Nichols and Son, 1844), Camden Society publication no. 30.
bibliographic.publisher: Printed for the Camden Society by John Bowyer Nichols and Son
bibliographic.title: The Thornton Romances: The Early English Metrical Romances of Perceval, Isumbras, Eglamour, and Degrevant, Selected from Manuscripts at Lincoln and Cambridge

entities.description: Halliwell's edited selection of four Middle English romances from three manuscripts at Lincoln and Cambridge, with an introduction, manuscript descriptions, an essay on terminal contractions, textual notes, and additional book matter.
entities.label: James Orchard Halliwell, The Thornton Romances (1844)
notes: CME00026 encodes the title-page content and printed pp. 1-256 only.

Halliwell describes Perceval and Isumbras as taken verbatim from Thornton's manuscript, but his terminal-contractions essay documents expansion and editorial judgment; 'verbatim' is therefore not treated as photographic or graphemically diplomatic reproduction.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1844 and is the public-domain source class described by U-M for this corpus; this statement is limited to the United States.
rights.component: 1844 printed edition, including Halliwell's editorial matter

entities.description: Robert Thornton's c. 1430-1450 paper miscellany. It is Halliwell's base witness for Perceval and Isumbras and his comparison witness for Eglamour and Degrevant.

holding.notes: Current component loci are Sir Isumbras ff. 109r-114v, Sir Degrevant ff. 130r-138v, Sir Eglamour ff. 138v-147r, and Sir Perceval ff. 161r-176r.

entities.label: Lincoln Cathedral Library MS 91

physicalDescription.extent: 322 leaves, apparently untrimmed, from an original minimum of 340; six paper leaves at front and three at back
physicalDescription.format: Composite literary, devotional, and medical codex in Robert Thornton's hand; current 1975 S. R. Cockerell binding
notes: The current catalog records damage or text loss at several leaves and fragments at ff. 108, 122, 133A, and 315-321.
The four CME00026 romances occupy separate ranges within a much larger 65-item miscellany.

rights.basis: The library offers charged digital images on request, but no terms for a not-yet-supplied complete object were available at item level.

entities.description: A mid-fifteenth-century paper miscellany written by two scribes. Halliwell printed Sir Eglamour from this manuscript and compared the Lincoln copy in his notes.

holding.notes: The current exact scholarly handlist gives Sir Eglamour at ff. 71r-79r; Halliwell and the XML use ff. 63r-71r. No current repository-owned item page resolving that crosswalk was located.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Ff.2.38

physicalDescription.extent: At least 261 folios in the current scholarly handlist
physicalDescription.format: Composite Middle English manuscript written by two scribes
notes: Sir Eglamour is one component among religious, didactic, romance, chronicle, and household texts.

rights.basis: CUL offers consultation, virtual viewing, and image-order enquiries, but no item-specific license for a not-yet-supplied complete object was found.
rights.component: Prospective Cambridge University Library-supplied Ff.2.38 photography

entities.description: The Findern manuscript, a paper household and literary miscellany produced c. 1446-1550 and associated with Findern, Derbyshire. Halliwell used its Sir Degrevant as copy text and collated the defective Lincoln witness.

holding.notes: Sir Degrevant is at CUDL-labeled ff. 80r-93v, canvases 163-190. The scholarly handlist records older foliation ff. 96r-109v.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Ff.1.6

physicalDescription.extent: Current digital sequence: covers, inner covers, flyleaves, ff. 1r-159v, rear leaves, back cover, and spine; the manuscript is defective at beginning and end and the handlist reports 29 missing leaves
physicalDescription.format: Household and literary miscellany associated with Findern, Derbyshire
notes: Direct visual inspection confirms Sir Degrevant begins on CUDL canvas 163 (label 80r) and ends on canvas 190 (label 93v).

reviewNotes: The four romances, the 1844 edited selection, the three physical witnesses, the U-M encoding, the checked-in XML, printed facsimiles, and digital reproductions are modeled as distinct layers.

Halliwell's own introduction and editorial essay control the base-text and collation statements. Current Database of Middle English Romance edition notes that call Thornton the base for Eglamour or Degrevant conflict with that primary evidence and are not propagated.

Ff.1.6 has competing foliation systems. The Halliwell/XML and CUDL image labels place Sir Degrevant at ff. 80r-93v (canvases 163-190); the scholarly handlist gives older foliation 96r-109v. Direct inspection of the cached incipit and explicit verifies that these locators refer to one physical text.

Completeness of the Ff.1.6 cache means all 329 canvases in the provider manifest, including covers, inner covers, flyleaves, every numbered leaf, back cover, and spine, at an access derivative profile; it is not a claim to preservation-master resolution or redistribution rights.

lineage.summary: CME00026 is the repository copy of a University of Michigan electronic selection from James Orchard Halliwell's 1844 Thornton Romances. The encoding retains the edition title page and the four verse texts on printed pp. 1-256, but omits Halliwell's introduction, manuscript descriptions, essay on terminal contractions, notes, and remaining volume matter. Halliwell printed Sir Perceval and Sir Isumbras from Lincoln Cathedral MS 91; Sir Eglamour from Cambridge University Library MS Ff.2.38 with Lincoln variants; and Sir Degrevant from Cambridge University Library MS Ff.1.6 with Lincoln variants. A complete edition scan and the complete 329-canvas Ff.1.6 digital object are cached. Lincoln exposes one unlabeled selected catalog image and a request route, while no complete public digital Ff.2.38 object was found.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English authors
catalogSummary.date: Underlying romances composed before 1320 through the early fifteenth century; edited selection published 1844
catalogSummary.region: Northeast Midlands of England, with Eglamour perhaps associated more specifically with Yorkshire; witness copying and publication places are recorded separately
catalogSummary.title: The Thornton Romances

catalogingSubject.label: The Thornton Romances
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The U-M/General Corpus edition-level selection encoded from James Orchard Halliwell's 1844 Camden Society volume: its title-page content and four complete verse texts on printed pp. 1-256, Sir Perceval of Galles, Sir Isumbras, Sir Eglamour of Artois, and Sir Degrevant. The selection excludes the council list, Halliwell's introduction and manuscript descriptions on pp. v-l, his essay on terminal contractions on pp. li-lvi, notes beginning on p. 257, and remaining volume matter. It is neither one medieval work, one manuscript, the complete 1844 publication, the U-M encoding, nor the checked-in XML artifact.

contentParts.label: The Romance of Sir Perceval of Galles
contentParts.summary: The sole surviving Middle English Perceval romance, tracing the hero from forest upbringing through Arthurian initiation, marriage, recognition, and crusading death.

contentParts.label: The Romance of Sir Isumbras
contentParts.summary: A religious romance in which a prosperous knight accepts suffering, loses and recovers his family, completes penance and pilgrimage, and converts conquered lands.

contentParts.label: The Romance of Sir Eglamour of Artois
notes: Lincoln MS 91 ff. 138v-147r is a collated comparison witness, not the copy text of Halliwell's printed Eglamour.
contentParts.summary: A chivalric romance of deadly bride-winning tasks, separation of lovers and child, pilgrimage, tournaments, recognition, and reunion.

contentParts.label: The Romance of Sir Degrevant

locators.value: Halliwell/XML and CUDL ff. 80r-93v, CUDL canvases 163-190; scholarly-handlist older foliation ff. 96r-109v

notes: Lincoln MS 91 ff. 130r-138v is a collated and defective comparison witness, not Halliwell's continuous copy text.
contentParts.summary: An Arthurian-associated chivalric romance of estate conflict, courtship across a family feud, tournaments, secret meetings, reconciliation, marriage, and crusading death.

assertion.notes: A specialist work-level range, kept distinct from the c. 1430-1450 production of the sole manuscript witness.

dateStatements.display: First half of the fourteenth century

assertion.notes: The approximate date is not converted into an invented exact normalized interval.
This is the selection's publication date, not a medieval composition date.

scope.description: First publication of Halliwell's four-romance edited selection

extent.description: The XML has five DIV1 elements: one title-page wrapper and four poem divisions. Across the four poems it has 446 LG and 6,329 L elements; there are 257 PB and 1,308 MILESTONE elements across the file. These are encoding metrics. Halliwell's printed and manuscript lineations broadly align, but the LG groups and headings remain markup-dependent rather than canonical stanza counts for every witness.

formStatements.description: All four selected medieval works are tail-rhyme verse romances. The encoded modern-English title page and excluded prose apparatus do not make the cataloged textual selection mixed-form.

assertion.notes: Brief Latin invocations and colophon phrases do not make the verse selection substantively multilingual.

scope.description: Title page, modern editorial headings, and excluded English apparatus that defines the selection

notes: The preferred title describes Halliwell's 1844 edited selection rather than one medieval work or a generic name for Lincoln MS 91.
The four medieval romances are modeled as stable content parts with separate composition, genre, subject, structure, and lineage scopes.
Robert Thornton's scribal and compilation responsibility remains witness-level; it is not authorship of Perceval, Isumbras, Eglamour, or Degrevant.
Halliwell's direct statements establish Ff.2.38 and Ff.1.6 as the copy texts for Eglamour and Degrevant, despite conflicting shorthand in a current scholarly database.
The record remains partial because complete Lincoln and Ff.2.38 images, the Ff.2.38 foliation crosswalk, detailed modern component scholarship, and the exact U-M production history remain unresolved.

next_steps: Ask CUL for the foliation history and a complete object including covers, spine, endleaves, blanks, every folio, and adjacent works.
Inspect the 1979 Scolar Press facsimile and current physical manuscript.
Retain both locator systems for one witness until a leaf-by-leaf crosswalk is documented.
openQuestions.question: How exactly do Halliwell/XML ff. 63r-71r for the Ff.2.38 Eglamour crosswalk to the current scholarly handlist's ff. 71r-79r, and can CUL supply a complete current digital object for direct verification?

next_steps: Request a complete physical-object surrogate from Lincoln Cathedral, not a four-romance excerpt.
Map all four work portions separately after receipt.
Compare Halliwell's brackets, italics, contraction expansions, omissions, and notes against exact leaves.
openQuestions.question: Can complete current MS 91 images verify Halliwell's Perceval and Isumbras readings and his Lincoln collations for Eglamour and Degrevant directly?

next_steps: Consult current critical editions and peer-reviewed studies for each romance separately.
Record materially competing claims part by part with attribution rather than averaging them.
Do not transfer a manuscript's production date or dialect to composition without explicit work-level argument.
openQuestions.question: Which competing modern composition dates, localizations, source analogues, and version histories should be represented for each of the four underlying romances beyond the current specialist handlist summaries?

next_steps: Retrieve the current exact U-M bitstream after the automated access challenge is removed or request it from U-M.
Compare bytes, identifiers, markup, and revision history.
Keep the 2016 date scoped to repository review rather than digital creation.
openQuestions.question: When and by whom was CME00026 first keyed, and how does the repository XML differ from U-M's current bulk XML?

assertion.notes: This is work-level linguistic and literary localization. It does not transfer Lincoln MS 91's North Yorkshire production or Findern's Derbyshire association to every composition stage.
Halliwell expressly warns that Robert Thornton was the scribe rather than author of his miscellany; current specialist records identify no author for these four romances.

scope.description: All four medieval romance parts collectively, without implying a single author
Selection and ordering of four romances from three manuscripts
The four printed copy texts, with witness-specific dependencies recorded in lineage
Lincoln variants reported against the two Cambridge copy texts

summaries.value: Four anonymous Middle English tail-rhyme romances follow knights and families through combat, tournaments, exile, loss, courtship, pilgrimage, and reunion. Perceval moves from forest innocence into Arthurian knighthood; Isumbras undergoes divinely imposed poverty and penance before recovering his family; Eglamour completes deadly tasks and reunites with Cristabel and their son; Degrevant defends his estates, wins Melidor, and reconciles with her father.

The XML contains one title-page division and four complete poem divisions: Perceval, 143 verse groups and 2,288 encoded lines on printed pp. 1-87; Isumbras, 68 groups and 794 lines on pp. 88-120; Eglamour, 116 groups and 1,341 lines on pp. 121-176; and Degrevant, 119 groups and 1,906 lines on pp. 177-256.

The record describes Halliwell's encoded edition-level selection. Perceval and Isumbras derive from Lincoln MS 91; Eglamour from CUL Ff.2.38 with Lincoln variants; and Degrevant from CUL Ff.1.6 with Lincoln variants. Halliwell's unencoded apparatus establishes these dependencies but is not itself part of CME00026's selected text.

Textual, physical, editorial, and digital layers are kept separate. Robert Thornton is a scribe and compiler of MS 91, not author of the romances. Halliwell is editor, selector, transcriber, and collator. Manuscript dates and locations do not become composition facts, and complete or partial image access is recorded at the exact facsimile layer.

assertion.notes: This is the edition-level selection title, not a shared medieval title for the four works and not evidence that Robert Thornton authored them.

titles.value: The Thornton Romances: The Early English Metrical Romances of Perceval, Isumbras, Eglamour, and Degrevant, Selected from Manuscripts at Lincoln and Cambridge
The Romance of Sir Perceval of Galles
The Romance of Sir Eglamour of Artois

work.author: Anonymous Middle English authors
work.dateDisplay: Underlying romances composed before 1320 through the early fifteenth century; edited selection published 1844
work.regionDisplay: Northeast Midlands of England, with Eglamour perhaps associated more specifically with Yorkshire; witness copying and publication places are recorded separately
work.summary: Four anonymous Middle English tail-rhyme romances follow knights and families through combat, tournaments, exile, loss, courtship, pilgrimage, and reunion. Perceval moves from forest innocence into Arthurian knighthood; Isumbras undergoes divinely imposed poverty and penance before recovering his family; Eglamour completes deadly tasks and reunites with Cristabel and their son; Degrevant defends his estates, wins Melidor, and reconciles with her father.
